title: 5 1 First order ODEs
body:
TOPIC 5 : ORDINARY DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
        5.1 FIRST ORDER DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS


                   SEPARABLE DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION
                       These differential equations are of the form
                                       dy
                                          = f (x)g(y).
                                       dx
  and are solved by separating x’s and y’s on each side of the equation and integrating.


                     LINEAR DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION
              The general first-order linear ordinary differential equation is
                                    dy
                                       + P (x)y = Q(x).
                                    dx
                                                       R
   If we evaluate the integrating factor R(x) = e P (x)dx then the solution is given by
                                          Z
                                       1
                                y=          R(x)Q(x) dx
                                     R(x)


An ordinary differential equation (often abbreviated ODE) is an equation relating a
function of a single variable to its (ordinary) derivatives. For example,

                         dy                                dy
             (1)            + y = x3             (2)          = x2 y 3
                         dx                                dx


                                                                               1
             (3)      y 00 + 6y 0 − 5y = 0       (4)       ẍ + 6ẋ − 5x =
                                                                             t2 + 5

The solution to an ODE is not a number, but a function. Just like integration, solving
the ODE can introduce unknown constants of integration that need to be determined
using additional information. The method of solution depends completely on the type of
equation you are dealing with. So an important skill is being able to classify ODEs:

   • The order of a differential equation is the biggest derivative that appears in the
     equation.

   • A differential equation is said to be linear if the unknown function y(x) and all its
     derivatives appear linearly. (The dependent variable x can do as it likes.)

Example 1 Find the order of each of the ODEs shown above and state whether each
is linear or not.



                                             1
 The method you use to solve a differential equation depends on the type of equation you
are dealing with. First order equations are attacked in a manner totally different from
second order equations and unfortunately many differential equations can’t be solved at
all. There are however some general rules.
• The solution to any ODE will always involve unknown constants. It is crucial to respect
the constants and take good care of them.
• The number of constants in the solution is always equal to order of the ODE.
• If an ODE is given with some initial conditions (i.c.’s), the i.c.’s are used to determine
values for the constants.

In this lecture we will look at the two main types of first order problems: separable and
linear.

            SEPARABLE DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
Separable differential equation are ODEs that can be written so that all the x’s are on
one side of the equation and all the y’s are on the other side. Solving the ODE then just
involves integrating both sides of the equation. Not all first order problems are separable!


Example 2 Solve
                                      dy
                                         = y(2x + 3)
                                      dx

A general first-order linear ODE can be written as

                                   dx

If we evaluate the integrating factor R(x) = e P (x)dx then the solution is given by

 Sometimes you are not actually given the ODE but have to construct it yourself. Keep in
mind that ODEs are all about rates of change. There might also be additional information
that you will use as initial conditions.

Example 8 The air in a 50 cubic metre room is initially clean. Chris lights up a
cigarette introducing smoke into the room’s air at a rate of 2 mg/minute. An air condi-
tioning system exchanges the mixture of air and smoke with clean air at a rate of 5 cubic
metres per minute. Assume that the smoke is mixed uniformly throughout the room. Let
S be the total amount of smoke in the room (in mg) and let t be the time (in minutes).
             dS          S
a) Show that      =2−       at S(0) = 0.
              dt         10            t
b) Hence show that S(t) = 20 − 20e(− 10 )
c) What is the level of smoke after 4 minutes?
